source: Syrian Arab Republic Ministry of Health Expanded Programme on Immunization (EPI); WHO/UNICEF JRF 2024 country profile; WHO EMRO Syria; UNICEF Syrian Arab Republic country pages
source_notes: Schedule composed from WHO/UNICEF JRF Syria 2024 country profile, WHO EMRO Syria EPI page, and the WHO Immunization Data portal for Syria (SYR). Syrian MoH uses a 2/4/6-month primary series (not the WHO 6/10/14-week schedule), more similar to Egypt's pattern than to most sub-Saharan African programmes. Coverage was high pre-2011 (~95%) and dropped substantially during the conflict; rebuilding ongoing under WHO/UNICEF support. Rotavirus and PCV are NOT in routine public EPI as of 2024 — they are paediatric-society recommendations / private-sector. IPV was introduced into routine schedule for polio endgame.
verification_notes: Phase 1 seed marked needs_review on the country selector. Do NOT treat as clinically authoritative until row-level reconciliation against the current WHO/XMart 2024 JRF schedule and Syrian MoH EPI bulletin is complete. Open items include: current IPV schedule (single fIPV vs 2-dose), HPV introduction status, PCV public-sector introduction, MR (Measles-Rubella) timing, area-specific coverage (NW Syria, NE Syria, regime-controlled areas may have different procurement chains).
source_year_or_release_date: 2024 WHO/UNICEF JRF schedule data; seed first added 2026-04-25
scope: Routine childhood immunisation, birth to 72 months (6 years). Compulsory (Syrian EPI) antigens.
context_note: Source-country schedule. In cross-border reconciliation INTO Egypt, Syrian-EPI doses are the reliable signal on a Syrian card (which may be in Arabic, English, or both). Syria and Egypt both use a 2/4/6-month primary series, so age-based dose timing aligns more closely than with the WHO 6/10/14-week countries. Rotavirus and PCV are NOT routine in either Syrian or Egyptian public EPI — absence on a Syrian card is expected, not a deficiency.
key_differences_vs_egypt: Syria and Egypt BOTH use a 2/4/6-month primary series — closer alignment than WHO 6/10/14-week countries.
Syria uses Pentavalent (DTPw-Hib-HepB, 5-in-1, no IPV) at 2/4/6 months; Egypt uses Hexavalent (6-in-1, includes IPV).
Syria gives separate IPV at 4 months (and per polio endgame may now have a second IPV); Egypt's IPV is bundled in Hexavalent.
Syria gives BCG at birth; Egypt gives BCG at 1 month — a Syrian-origin BCG dose still satisfies the Egyptian BCG requirement.
Syria gives HepB monovalent at birth; Egypt also gives HepB at birth — alignment.
Syria gives Measles-containing dose at 9 months and 18 months; Egypt gives MMR at 12 months and 18 months — Mumps and Rubella coverage may need to be verified for the 9-month Syrian dose.
Neither Syria nor Egypt has routine Rotavirus or PCV in public EPI — absence is not a gap.
Yellow Fever is NOT in the Syrian schedule (Syria is YF-non-endemic) — alignment with Egypt.
Booster DPT/OPV at 18 months in Syria; Egypt has booster DTP + MMR2 + OPV at 18 months — broadly aligned.

doses.notes: At birth or as soon as possible.
doses.verification_source: WHO/UNICEF JRF Syria 2024; WHO EMRO Syria

doses.notes: Birth dose (HBV0). Standalone monovalent HepB at birth.
OPV0 at birth. Syria uses bivalent OPV (bOPV) in routine schedule.
Penta1 at 2 months. 5-in-1 covering DTPw, Hib, HepB. Does NOT include IPV — IPV is given separately.
IPV1 at 4 months. Syria introduced IPV into routine schedule for polio endgame. Single IPV dose; second IPV under WHO consideration.
doses.verification_source: WHO/UNICEF JRF Syria 2024; WHO Polio Eradication Initiative

doses.notes: Penta3 at 6 months. Primary DTPw-Hib-HepB series complete.
Measles first dose (MCV1) at 9 months. May be measles-monovalent or MR depending on year and area; verify against the card.
MMR (or measles-containing booster) at 18 months. Older Syrian schedules pre-2010 may show measles-monovalent at 18 months instead of MMR; newer cards typically show MMR.
DPT booster (4th DPT-containing dose) at 18 months.

open_questions: Current IPV schedule — single fIPV vs second IPV dose introduction status.
PCV public-sector introduction status — historically not in EPI; check most recent FMOH bulletin.
Rotavirus introduction status — historically not in EPI.
MR (Measles-Rubella) timing at 9 vs 18 months — varies by year and area.
Area-specific coverage — NW Syria (Idlib), NE Syria, and regime-controlled areas may have different procurement chains and coverage.
